records.title: INTRODUCTION
prefer: The application of the principles and rules described in this Chapter has been devised to lead to a preferred IUPAC name.

exceptions.condition: However, such deviations should not be done without good reason, and names so derived are not recommended for general use in publications

not necessarily the aim of a practicing chemist who wants to communicate with his colleagues in familiar, well understood terms, but it may be appropriate to those who cite chemical names in legislative documents, in international trade and commerce, or for preparing programs for index purposes, databases, and retrieval systems

records.title: INTRODUCTION
prefer: The three seniority orders, for classes, rings and ring systems, and the principal chain, are expressed in a general seniority order called ‘seniority order for parent structures’.
A thorough revision and extension of the seniority order of classes, of rings and ring systems, and for selecting the principal chain was needed in the context of preferred names.
In acyclic parent structures the order of seniority between unsaturation and length of chain given in earlier recommendations is reversed.
Thus, the first criterion to be considered in choosing a preferred parent acyclic chain is the length of the chain; unsaturation is now the second criterion.
The criteria necessary to select a preferred IUPAC name are described in P-45; thus, traditional criteria related to substituent groups are not incorporated in this Section.
examples: A thorough revision and extension of the seniority order of classes, of rings and ring systems, and for selecting the principal chain was needed in the context of preferred names
The criteria necessary to select a preferred IUPAC name are described in P-45
records.source_quote: The selection of a preferred parent structure is based on the seniority of classes (see P-41), which gives priority first to characteristic groups expressed as suffixes and then to parent hydrides when different classes are present.

records.title: Polycyclic systems. The senior polycyclic ring system occurs first in the following list of polycyclic ring system types:
prefer: candidate where polycyclic systems. The senior polycyclic ring system occurs first in the following list of polycyclic ring system types:
The seniority order between parent hydrides having the same number of identical heteroatoms, the same number of rings, and the same number of skeletal atoms is a change from previous practice.

Seniority of polycyclic ring systems is now facilitated by a hierarchical order of ring systems, which includes cyclic and acyclic phane systems and ranks all ring systems in the order given in this recommendation.

records.title: Linear (acyclic) phanes
prefer: Accordingly, the seniority criteria for linear phane systems given below are applied successively until no alternatives remain.
tables_or_terms: chalcogen seniority: O > S > Se > Te
halogen seniority: F > Cl > Br > I
acyclic heteroatom seniority: O > S > Se > Te > N > P > As > Sb > Bi > Si > Ge > Sn > Pb > B > Al > Ga > In > Tl
ring-assembly heteroatom seniority: F > Cl > Br > I > O > S > Se > Te > N > P > As > Sb > Bi > Si > Ge > Sn > Pb > B > Al > Ga > In > Tl
records.source_quote: Although linear phane systems can be viewed as heteroacyclic chains, in which the amplificants are heteroatoms and the connecting atoms or chains are carbon atoms, seniority for linear (acyclic) phanes follows closely the criteria used for cyclic phane systems (see P-44.2.2.2.2).
